text: Grafnan again and this is just robotic inguinal lymph node dissection actually minimally invasive surgery for inguinal lymph node dissection was first used by Dargent and he did about ten to fifteen cases laparoscopically but subsequently for whatever reason he stopped doing that this was in early eighties it was then picked up by the urologists and they started doing a Brazilian group started doing laparoscopic inguinal lymphadenectomy for penile cancers It is the urologist actually who have the largest series of inguinal lymph node dissections. But in our hospital, we too are doing it for vulval cancers. We started with laparoscopic inguinal lymph node dissection, but now we've graduated onto robotic inguinal lymph node dissections. And this is done for very select cases. Preferably, where the inguinal lymph nodes are not palpably enlarged, or if they are palpable, maybe sub-centimeter or something. That's one. Secondly, we make it a point to send the specimen for frozen section. If in the frozen section it is positive, any lymph node is positive, it is mandatory to do an open dissection for them, so that you should not have left behind any lymph nodes. So we come to the conclusion that then why are we doing this at all? Basically to reduce the morbidity. All of us know how much morbidity is involved in inguinal lymph node dissection and how the patients have wound dehiscence and necrosis and lymphoria and what have you. So I'll just quickly show you a video of robotic inguinal lymph node dissection and we'll take it from there.

The patient is positioned supine with legs abducted, and the femoral triangle is marked, showing the anterior superior iliac spine and pubic tubercle. The camera port is placed four centimeters below the apex of the femoral triangle, with two robotic ports positioned six centimeters superior medially and laterally from the camera port. The assistant port is visible, used for manipulating lymph nodes. The robotic system is docked parallel to the limb on the lateral side, with the assistant standing nearby. The camera port and two robotic arms, equipped with monopolar and bipolar instruments, are clearly visible.

The surgeon creates a pneumoperitoneum, visually resembling a frog's back, by making a space with a finger. The surgical team palpates the area to ensure the dissection remains just above Scarpa's fascia, avoiding thinning of the skin. The retrograde dissection proceeds from below upwards along the saphenous vein towards the femoral vessels.

The surgeon navigates within the femoral triangle, identifying and clipping an accessory saphenous vein using a clip from the assistant port. The medial border is defined by the adductor muscle, while the lateral border is the sartorius muscle. The surgeon carefully dissects around named branches from the inguinal ligament, including the superficial external pudendal and superficial circumflex iliac arteries, ensuring the dissection remains in the correct plane without approaching too close to the skin.

The surgeon identifies and separates the superficial vessels, including the superficial circumflex iliac and superficial external pudendal branches. Fatty tissue is carefully dissected to ensure that lymph nodes are included with the specimen and not left behind.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector for the inguinal lymph node dissection, ensuring the skin thickness is adequate to prevent necrosis. All vessels are clipped, and the pressure is maintained at eight mm in the confined surgical space.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the border of the muscle, removing fibro fatty tissue. A small branch arising from the femoral artery is visible, pulsating as it is exposed.

And for the vulva, we know that there's no such thing as doing only superficial lymph nodes. We always do the deep femorals as well unlike what they do for penile cancers over there they first do the superficial inguinal lymph nodes they send it for frozen if positive only then do they dissect out the deep inguinal nodes whereas for vulval cancers we always do both sets of nodes because some for some patients the vulva drains directly into the deep nodes. Certain areas of the vulva drain directly into the deep nodes and in about as high as about seven to eight percent of the cases you might have a deep node which is positive and the superficial inguinal nodes might be negative. So, therefore, it is important that we dissect out the deep inguinal lymph nodes as well.
The surgeon approaches the saphenous vein during the dissection, carefully working to preserve the vein while performing the inguinal lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon clips and cuts the saphenous vein at its opening into the femoral vein. A gauze is placed for hemostasis and field clearance, visible above the inguinal ligament within the surgical triangle. The dissection appears nearly complete.

The surgeon ligates the saphenous vein at multiple locations, encountering and ligating the accessory saphenous vein as well. A clip appears to slip, prompting the use of a suture to secure the vessel. The surgical field is visible, showing the femoral vein and completed dissection.
The surgeon reduces the pressure to check for any remaining bleeding before concluding the surgery. The specimen is extracted through the system port, completing the procedure.
